reference_answer_corrected: Take any deterministic constrained-efficient allocation with prices (p1,p2) and quantities (q1,q2) (equivalently shares) under the efficient-rationing rule, so total sales equal q1+q2 and are allocated to the low-price firm first. Assume wlog p1<p2. Then all consumers with willingness to pay ≥p2 are willing to buy from firm 2 at p2, and firm 2 sells q2 = max{0, D(p2) − q1}, so feasibility requires q1 ≤ D(p1) and q1+q2 = D(p2) (since any demand at p2 not served by firm 1 is served by firm 2). Now increase firm 1’s price to p1′=p2, keeping quantities fixed at (q1,q2). This is feasible because q1 ≤ D(p1) and D is downward sloping, so D(p2) ≤ D(p1) implies q1 ≤ D(p2)=D(p1′). Under efficient rationing with equal prices, firm 1 can still sell q1 and firm 2 sells the residual q2. Firm 2’s profit is unchanged (same p2 and same q2), so the constraint Π2≥\bar{Π}^2 remains satisfied. Firm 1’s profit weakly increases because its unit margin rises from (p1−c1) to (p2−c1) while its quantity stays q1. Hence any allocation with p1<p2 cannot be constrained efficient. A symmetric argument rules out p2<p1. Therefore any deterministic constrained-efficient market-sharing allocation must have identical prices: p1=p2.
reference_answer_latex_from_by_problem:
(i) Let $\Phi_i(p)\equiv(p-c_i)D(p)$. Note that $\Phi_i$ is (by assumption) concave, increases up to the monopoly price $p_1^m$, and decreases thereafter. After substituting $s_1$, one gets
\[
\Pi^1=\max_{p}\ \Phi_1(p)\left(1-\frac{\bar{\Pi}^2}{\Phi_2(p)}\right).
\]
The first-order condition is
\[
\Phi_1'(p)\left(1-\frac{\bar{\Pi}^2}{\Phi_2(p)}\right)+\Phi_1(p)\frac{\bar{\Pi}^2\Phi_2'(p)}{\Phi_2^{2}(p)}=0.
\]
This implies that $\Phi_1'$ and $\Phi_2'$ have opposite signs. Because $p_1^m<p_2^m$, we have $\Phi_1'\le 0\le \Phi_2'$, and $p_1^m\le p\le p_2^m$. The second-order derivative of the objective function is
\[
\Phi_1''(p)\left(1-\frac{\bar{\Pi}^2}{\Phi_2(p)}\right)
+\frac{\Phi_1'(p)\bar{\Pi}^2\Phi_2'(p)}{\Phi_2^{2}(p)}
-\frac{2\Phi_1(p)\bar{\Pi}^2(\Phi_2'(p))^2}{\Phi_2^{3}(p)}
+\frac{2\Phi_1(p)\bar{\Pi}^2\Phi_2''(p)}{\Phi_2^{2}(p)}.
\]
The first three terms in this expression are negative. The fourth term is negative when the first-order condition is satisfied. Hence, the objective function is quasi-concave, and we do have an optimum.

(ii) This is straightforward.

(iii) Take the derivative of the first-order condition with respect to $p$ and $\bar{\Pi}^2$. Writing
\[
s_2=-\frac{(p-c_2)\Phi_1'(p)}{(c_2-c_1)D(p)}
\]
and taking the derivative shows that $s_2$ is an increasing function of $p$ and therefore of $\bar{\Pi}^2$. When the profit target for firm 2 is zero, the highest profit for firm 1 is its monopoly profit and is obtained for $p=p^m(c_1)$ and $s_2=0$. Conversely, in order for $\bar{\Pi}^2=\Pi^m(c_2)$ to be obtained, the price must be $p=p^m(c_2)$, and firm 2 must serve the whole market. More generally, there is a trade-off between aggregate efficiency (which requires $p=p^m(c_1)$ and $s_2=0$) and sharing of profits. The higher the profit target for firm 2, the higher the market price and the higher firm 2's market share. See figure 6.7.

(iv) From the envelope theorem,
\[
\frac{d\Pi^1}{d\bar{\Pi}^2}=-\frac{\Phi_1(p)}{\Phi_2(p)}.
\]
Using the chain rule,
\[
\frac{d^2\Pi^1}{d\bar{\Pi}^2}
=\left(\frac{-\Phi_2(p)\Phi_1'(p)+\Phi_1(p)\Phi_2'(p)}{\Phi_2^{2}(p)}\right)\frac{dp}{d\bar{\Pi}^2}>0,
\]
because
\[
\Phi_1'(p)\le 0\le \Phi_2'(p),
\]
and from question iii,
\[
\frac{dp}{d\bar{\Pi}^2}>0.
\]

Schmalensee (1987) uses axiomatic bargaining theory to select a point on this convex Pareto frontier. He observes that, if the leading firm's cost advantage is substantial, its likely gains from collusion are relatively small. (In the extreme case, if the low-cost firm's monopoly price is lower than its rival's marginal cost, the low-cost firm cannot gain from colluding.)

(v) Question iv implies that our ``efficient market-sharing allocation'' is optimal only in the class of \emph{deterministic} allocations. The firms could obtain higher expected payoffs by ``tossing a coin'' to decide who will be the monopolist. More formally, the firms could achieve any point on the straight line between A and B in figure 6.7 by letting one of them be a monopolist depending on the realization of a random variable. Alternatively, in a repeated-game context with little impatience (so that the firms' objective is approximately the average of their profits---see section 6.3), the firms could take turns being the monopolist.

(vi) Suppose that firm 1 sells $q_1$ at price $p_1<p_2$. Firm 2's residual demand is $D(p_2)-q_1$ under efficient rationing. This demand would be left unaffected if firm 1 raised its price. Hence, firm 1 could continue selling $q_1$ at a price higher than $p_1$ without hurting firm 2.
reference_answer_original: (vi) Suppose that firm 1 sells $q_1$ at price $p_1<p_2$. Firm 2's residual demand is $D(p_2)-q_1$ under efficient rationing. This demand would be left unaffected if firm 1 raised its price. Hence, firm 1 could continue selling $q_1$ at a price higher than $p_1$ without hurting firm 2.
reference_reasoning: Take any deterministic constrained-efficient allocation with prices (p1,p2) and quantities (q1,q2) under the efficient-rationing rule. WLOG assume p1<p2. Then consumers buy from firm 1 first at p1 up to q1, and any remaining consumers with willingness to pay at least p2 buy from firm 2 at p2. Feasibility implies q1<=D(p1) and total sales at prices (p1,p2) equal D(p2), so q1+q2=D(p2) and q2=max(0,D(p2)-q1). Now raise firm 1's price to p1'=p2 while keeping quantities fixed at (q1,q2). Because demand is downward sloping and p2>p1, we have D(p2)<=D(p1), hence q1<=D(p1) implies q1<=D(p2)=D(p1'). Thus the same quantities remain feasible under efficient rationing with equal prices: firm 1 sells q1 and firm 2 sells the residual q2. Firm 2's profit is unchanged (same price p2 and same quantity q2), so the constraint Pi2>=barPi2 still holds. Firm 1's profit weakly increases because its unit margin rises from (p1-c1) to (p2-c1) while its quantity stays q1. Therefore any allocation with p1<p2 cannot be constrained efficient. By symmetry, p2<p1 is also impossible. Hence any deterministic constrained-efficient market-sharing allocation must have p1=p2.

source_problem_latex:
Two firms produce a homogeneous good with constant unit costs $c_1<c_2$.
Market demand is $D(p)$ (assume $D$ is continuous and downward sloping on the relevant range).
For any unit cost $c$, let $p^m(c)$ denote the monopoly price solving
\[
p^m(c)\in\arg\max_p (p-c)D(p).
\]
Side payments (lump-sum transfers) between firms are prohibited.

If the two firms could legally sign a contract with transfers, they would let firm 1 produce
everything and charge $p^m(c_1)$, then split profits using a transfer. Since transfers are illegal,
we instead study \emph{constrained efficient} profit-sharing agreements without transfers in which both
firms charge the same price $p$ and choose market shares $s_1,s_2$ with $s_1+s_2=1$.

Interpretation: firm $i$ produces exactly $q_i=s_iD(p)$. If some consumers are rationed at one firm,
they purchase from the other firm at the same price $p$ (so total demand served is $D(p)$).

Fix a profit target $\bar{\Pi}^2\in[0,\Pi^m(c_2)]$ for firm 2. The constrained efficient allocation
chooses $p,s_1,s_2$ to maximize firm 1's profit subject to firm 2 receiving at least $\bar{\Pi}^2$:
\begin{align}
\max_{(p,s_1,s_2)}\quad \Pi^1 &= (p-c_1)s_1D(p) \tag{6.1}\\
\text{s.t.}\quad \Pi^2 &= (p-c_2)s_2D(p)\ge \bar{\Pi}^2,\quad s_1+s_2=1. \notag
\end{align}
Suppose that the profit function $(p-c)D(p)$ is concave for all $p$ and $c$.

\begin{enumerate}
\item[(i)] After substituting $s_1=1-s_2$ and using the constraint $\Pi^2\ge\bar{\Pi}^2$ to eliminate
(or bound) $s_2$, obtain the first-order condition for the optimal price $p$.
Show that
\[
p^m(c_1)\le p\le p^m(c_2).
\]
Show that the objective function (in the reduced-form problem) is quasi-concave.

\item[(ii)] Show that the first-order condition can be written as
\[
\bigl[(p-c_1)D'(p)+D(p)\bigr]+\frac{(c_2-c_1)\bar{\Pi}^2}{(p-c_2)^2}=0.
\]

\item[(iii)] Conclude that as $\bar{\Pi}^2$ grows, both $p$ and $s_2$ grow.

\item[(iv)] Show that the Pareto frontier (the set of constrained efficient payoff pairs
$(\Pi^1,\Pi^2)$) is convex.

\item[(v)] What do you conclude from (iv)?

\item[(vi)] Now allow the firms to choose potentially different prices $(p_1,p_2)$, but keep the same
``efficient rationing'' rule: consumers always attempt to buy from the lower-priced firm first; if they
are rationed there (because that firm supplies less than demand at its price), they then buy from the
higher-priced firm at its price whenever they are willing to pay it. Under this rationing rule, show
that any \emph{deterministic} constrained efficient market-sharing allocation implies identical prices
for the two firms (i.e., $p_1=p_2$).
\end{enumerate}
split_answer: Take any deterministic constrained-efficient allocation with prices (p1,p2) and quantities (q1,q2) (equivalently shares) under the efficient-rationing rule, so total sales equal q1+q2 and are allocated to the low-price firm first. Assume wlog p1<p2. Then all consumers with willingness to pay ≥p2 are willing to buy from firm 2 at p2, and firm 2 sells q2 = max{0, D(p2) − q1}, so feasibility requires q1 ≤ D(p1) and q1+q2 = D(p2) (since any demand at p2 not served by firm 1 is served by firm 2). Now increase firm 1’s price to p1′=p2, keeping quantities fixed at (q1,q2). This is feasible because q1 ≤ D(p1) and D is downward sloping, so D(p2) ≤ D(p1) implies q1 ≤ D(p2)=D(p1′). Under efficient rationing with equal prices, firm 1 can still sell q1 and firm 2 sells the residual q2. Firm 2’s profit is unchanged (same p2 and same q2), so the constraint Π2≥\bar{Π}^2 remains satisfied. Firm 1’s profit weakly increases because its unit margin rises from (p1−c1) to (p2−c1) while its quantity stays q1. Hence any allocation with p1<p2 cannot be constrained efficient. A symmetric argument rules out p2<p1. Therefore any deterministic constrained-efficient market-sharing allocation must have identical prices: p1=p2.

before_annotation.answer:
(All payoffs are multiplied by 36.)

\[
V_6 = V_5 = V_4 = 9 + \delta\bigl[4.5/(1-\delta)\bigr]
      = 4.5\left(\frac{2-\delta}{1-\delta}\right).
\]

\[
V_3 = 4.5/(1-\delta) = W_3.
\]

\[
V_2 = 5 + \delta W_1.
\]

\[
V_1 = V_0 = \frac{\delta}{1-\delta}\,4.5
      = W_6 = W_5 = W_4 = W_0.
\]

\[
W_2 = \frac{\delta^2}{1-\delta}\,4.5.
\]

\[
W_1 = \alpha\left(5+\frac{\delta}{1-\delta}\,4.5\right)
      + (1-\alpha)\left(2.5+\frac{\delta^2}{1-\delta}\,4.5\right).
\]

The probability $\alpha$ is such that $V_1 = 2.5 + \delta W_1$ (each firm is indifferent between staying at $p_1$ and relenting to the monopoly price). Thus,
\[
\alpha = (4\delta + 9\delta^2 - 5)/(5\delta + 9\delta^2)
\]
(that is, $\alpha$ is approximately equal to $\frac{4}{7}$ for $\delta$ close to 1).

Checking that the strategies form an equilibrium is a bit tedious. We saw in the text that undercutting from $p_3$ to $p_2$ is not profitable. Let us simply show that at $p_2$ a firm prefers to undercut to $p_1$ rather than to revert to the monopoly price. By undercutting, it gets
\[
5+\delta W_1 = 5+(V_1-2.5)
\]
\[
\qquad\qquad = 2.5+V_1 = 2.5+\frac{\delta}{1-\delta}\,4.5
        > \frac{\delta}{1-\delta}\,4.5,
\]
which is what it would get by relenting.
before_annotation.question:
Consider the alternating-move price game with two identical firms, discount factor \(\delta \in (0,1)\), demand
\[
D(p)=1-p,
\]
marginal cost \(c=0\), and discrete price grid
\[
p_h=\frac{h}{6}, \qquad h=0,1,\ldots,6.
\]
Thus \(p_0=0\) is the competitive price and \(p_3=\frac12\) is the monopoly price. Each period, only one firm chooses a price; the other firm's price is the price chosen in the preceding period. If a firm charges a lower price than its rival, it gets the whole market; if both firms charge the same price, they split the market equally; if it charges a higher price, it sells nothing. Hence, if \(\Pi(p_k,p_h)\) denotes the current profit of a firm that charges \(p_k\) when its rival charges \(p_h\), then
\[
36\,\Pi(p_k,p_h)=
\begin{cases}
k(6-k), & k<h,\\[2mm]
\dfrac{h(6-h)}{2}, & k=h,\\[2mm]
0, & k>h.
\end{cases}
\]

Assume symmetric Markov reaction functions \(R_1(\cdot)=R_2(\cdot)=R(\cdot)\) given by
\[
R(p_6)=R(p_5)=R(p_4)=R(p_3)=R(p_0)=p_3,\qquad R(p_2)=p_1,
\]
and when the current state is \(p_1\),
\[
R(p_1)=
\begin{cases}
p_3 & \text{with probability }\alpha,\\
p_1 & \text{with probability }1-\alpha,
\end{cases}
\]
where \(\alpha\) may depend on \(\delta\).

For each \(h=0,1,\ldots,6\), let \(V_h\) be the discounted value of the firm whose turn it is to choose a price when the other firm chose \(p_h\) in the preceding period, and let \(W_h\) be the discounted value of its rival in that same state.

Compute the valuation functions \(\{V_h,W_h\}_{h=0}^6\), compute \(\alpha\), and check that the above strategies are an equilibrium for \(\delta\) close to \(1\).

effective_after_annotation.reference_reasoning: Work in payoffs multiplied by 36 as in the stem. At state p1, the mover mixes between p3 and p1, so it must be indifferent. If choose p3 against p1: current profit is 0 (since p3>p1) and continuation is δ W_3. If choose p1 against p1: current profit is 2.5 and continuation is δ W_1. Indifference: δ W_3 = 2.5 + δ W_1. From the given reaction function, state p3 leads to perpetual p3 alternation, giving W_3 = 4.5*delta/(1-delta^2). Also W_1 = delta V_1 (the non-mover’s value is next period’s mover value). Thus δ W_3 = 2.5 + δ^2 V_1, so V_1 = δ W_3 - 2.5 = 4.5*delta^2/(1-delta^2) - 2.5. By the mixing definition at state p1: V_1 = α(δ W_3) + (1-α)(2.5 + δ W_1) = α(δ W_3) + (1-α)(2.5 + δ^2 V_1). Using δ W_3 = 2.5 + δ^2 V_1 and solving for α yields α = (4*delta^2 + 9*delta^3 - 5)/(5*delta^2 + 9*delta^3).

question_original:
Consider the alternating-move price game with two identical firms, discount factor \(\delta \in (0,1)\), demand
\[
D(p)=1-p,
\]
marginal cost \(c=0\), and discrete price grid
\[
p_h=\frac{h}{6}, \qquad h=0,1,\ldots,6.
\]
Thus \(p_0=0\) is the competitive price and \(p_3=\frac12\) is the monopoly price. Each period, only one firm chooses a price; the other firm's price is the price chosen in the preceding period. If a firm charges a lower price than its rival, it gets the whole market; if both firms charge the same price, they split the market equally; if it charges a higher price, it sells nothing. Hence, if \(\Pi(p_k,p_h)\) denotes the current profit of a firm that charges \(p_k\) when its rival charges \(p_h\), then
\[
36\,\Pi(p_k,p_h)=
\begin{cases}
k(6-k), & k<h,\\[2mm]
\dfrac{h(6-h)}{2}, & k=h,\\[2mm]
0, & k>h.
\end{cases}
\]

Assume symmetric Markov reaction functions \(R_1(\cdot)=R_2(\cdot)=R(\cdot)\) given by
\[
R(p_6)=R(p_5)=R(p_4)=R(p_3)=R(p_0)=p_3,\qquad R(p_2)=p_1,
\]
and when the current state is \(p_1\),
\[
R(p_1)=
\begin{cases}
p_3 & \text{with probability }\alpha,\\
p_1 & \text{with probability }1-\alpha,
\end{cases}
\]
where \(\alpha\) may depend on \(\delta\).

For each \(h=0,1,\ldots,6\), let \(V_h\) be the discounted value of the firm whose turn it is to choose a price when the other firm chose \(p_h\) in the preceding period, and let \(W_h\) be the discounted value of its rival in that same state.

Compute the valuation functions \(\{V_h,W_h\}_{h=0}^6\), compute \(\alpha\), and check that the above strategies are an equilibrium for \(\delta\) close to \(1\).

reference_answer_corrected: (All payoffs below are multiplied by 36, as in the question.) Stage profits (×36): - If choose p_k<p_h: k(6−k). - If choose p_k=p_h: h(6−h)/2. - If choose p_k>p_h: 0. In particular: π(p3,p3)=9/2=4.5, π(p3,p6)=9, π(p1,p2)=5, π(p1,p1)=2.5. Let V_h be the value of the mover when last period’s price was p_h, and W_h the value of the non-mover in that same state. Because next period the other firm moves with state equal to the mover’s chosen price, we have for any state h: - If the mover chooses p_k, then V_h = Π(p_k,p_h) + δ W_k. - And W_h = δ V_h (the non-mover gets 0 today and becomes mover next period in the same state). Given the Markov reaction function R: R(p6)=R(p5)=R(p4)=R(p3)=R(p0)=p3, R(p2)=p1, and at p1: R(p1)=p3 w.p. α and p1 w.p. 1−α. 1) Values for states h∈{6,5,4,0}: Mover plays p3 against p_h, so V_h = 9 + δ W_3. At state 3, mover plays p3 against p3, so V_3 = 4.5 + δ W_3. Also W_3 = δ V_3. Solve: V_3 = 4.5 + δ(δ V_3) ⇒ V_3 = 4.5/(1−δ^2), and W_3 = δ V_3 = 4.5δ/(1−δ^2). Hence for h∈{6,5,4,0}: V_h = 9 + δW_3 = 9 + 4.5δ^2/(1−δ^2) = (9−4.5δ^2)/(1−δ^2), W_h = δV_h = δ(9−4.5δ^2)/(1−δ^2). 2) State h=2: Mover plays p1, so V_2 = 5 + δ W_1, W_2 = δ V_2. 3) State h=1: Mover randomizes: choose p3 w.p. α, p1 w.p. 1−α. If choose p3: current profit 0 (since p3>p1), next state 3 ⇒ continuation δW_3. If choose p1: current profit 2.5, next state 1 ⇒ continuation δW_1. Thus V_1 = α(0+δW_3) + (1−α)(2.5+δW_1). Also W_1 = δV_1. Compute α from indifference at state 1 between choosing p3 and p1: δW_3 = 2.5 + δW_1. Using W_3=4.5δ/(1−δ^2) and W_1=δV_1, and the indifference condition implies V_1 = δW_3 − 2.5 = 4.5δ^2/(1−δ^2) − 2.5. Plug into the expression for V_1: V_1 = α(δW_3) + (1−α)(2.5+δW_1) = α(δW_3) + (1−α)(2.5+δ^2V_1). Solving for α yields α = (4δ^2 + 9δ^3 − 5)/(5δ^2 + 9δ^3). (So α→8/14=4/7 as δ→1.) Then V_1 = 4.5δ^2/(1−δ^2) − 2.5, W_1 = δV_1 = 4.5δ^3/(1−δ^2) − 2.5δ. 4) State h=2 values explicitly: V_2 = 5 + δW_1 = 5 + δ(4.5δ^3/(1−δ^2) − 2.5δ) = 5 − 2.5δ^2 + 4.5δ^4/(1−δ^2), W_2 = δV_2. Summary (×36): V_3 = 4.5/(1−δ^2), W_3 = 4.5δ/(1−δ^2). For h∈{6,5,4,0}: V_h = 9 + 4.5δ^2/(1−δ^2), W_h = δV_h. V_1 = 4.5δ^2/(1−δ^2) − 2.5, W_1 = δV_1. V_2 = 5 + δW_1, W_2 = δV_2. Equilibrium check for δ close to 1: - At h∈{6,5,4,0}, deviating to any p_k≠p3 gives either lower current profit (if k<h but k≠3) or zero current profit (if k>h), while continuation is at most δW_3; for δ near 1, p3 maximizes. - At h=3, standard no-undercutting condition holds for δ near 1: deviating to p2 yields 8 + δW_2, while sticking to p3 yields 4.5 + δW_3; with the above values, for δ sufficiently close to 1, 4.5+δW_3 ≥ 8+δW_2. - At h=2, best response is p1: choosing p3 gives 0+δW_3, choosing p2 gives 2+δW_2, choosing p1 gives 5+δW_1; for δ near 1, 5+δW_1 is largest. - At h=1, α is chosen to satisfy δW_3 = 2.5+δW_1, so the mover is indifferent between p3 and p1 and hence mixing is optimal. Therefore, for δ sufficiently close to 1, the stated Markov strategies with α as above constitute a symmetric Markov perfect equilibrium.
reference_answer_latex_from_by_problem:
(All payoffs are multiplied by 36.)

\[
V_6 = V_5 = V_4 = 9 + \delta\bigl[4.5/(1-\delta)\bigr]
      = 4.5\left(\frac{2-\delta}{1-\delta}\right).
\]

\[
V_3 = 4.5/(1-\delta) = W_3.
\]

\[
V_2 = 5 + \delta W_1.
\]

\[
V_1 = V_0 = \frac{\delta}{1-\delta}\,4.5
      = W_6 = W_5 = W_4 = W_0.
\]

\[
W_2 = \frac{\delta^2}{1-\delta}\,4.5.
\]

\[
W_1 = \alpha\left(5+\frac{\delta}{1-\delta}\,4.5\right)
      + (1-\alpha)\left(2.5+\frac{\delta^2}{1-\delta}\,4.5\right).
\]

The probability $\alpha$ is such that $V_1 = 2.5 + \delta W_1$ (each firm is indifferent between staying at $p_1$ and relenting to the monopoly price). Thus,
\[
\alpha = (4\delta + 9\delta^2 - 5)/(5\delta + 9\delta^2)
\]
(that is, $\alpha$ is approximately equal to $\frac{4}{7}$ for $\delta$ close to 1).

Checking that the strategies form an equilibrium is a bit tedious. We saw in the text that undercutting from $p_3$ to $p_2$ is not profitable. Let us simply show that at $p_2$ a firm prefers to undercut to $p_1$ rather than to revert to the monopoly price. By undercutting, it gets
\[
5+\delta W_1 = 5+(V_1-2.5)
\]
\[
\qquad\qquad = 2.5+V_1 = 2.5+\frac{\delta}{1-\delta}\,4.5
        > \frac{\delta}{1-\delta}\,4.5,
\]
which is what it would get by relenting.

before_annotation.answer:
We content ourselves with showing that any payoff $(\Pi^1,\Pi^2)$ can be approximated as closely as desired for $\delta$ close to 1. Choose a price $p$ in $[c,p^m]$ such that $\Pi(p)=\Pi^1+\Pi^2$, and let $\Pi^1=\alpha\Pi(p)$ and $\Pi^2=(1-\alpha)\Pi(p)$.

Consider the ratio $\alpha/(1-\alpha)$. We know that any real number can be approximated as closely as is wished by a rational number. Let $m/n$ denote a rational approximation of $\alpha/(1-\alpha)$. Consider the following strategies: ``During the first $m$ periods, firm 1 charges $p$ and firm 2 charges a price strictly exceeding $p$; for the $n$ subsequent periods, firm 2 charges $p$ and firm 1 charges a price strictly exceeding $p$; during the $m$ subsequent periods, it is firm 1's turn to have the market at price $p$; and so forth. If anyone deviates, the firms charge the marginal cost for ever.'' These strategies clearly form an equilibrium for $\delta$ close to 1. Furthermore, the per-period payoff for firm 1 is
\begin{align*}
(1-\delta)\Pi(p)\big[(1+\delta+\cdots+\delta^{m-1})+(\delta^{m+n}+\cdots+\delta^{2m+n-1})+\cdots\big]\\
=\frac{1+\delta+\cdots+\delta^{m-1}}{1+\delta+\cdots+\delta^{m+n-1}}\Pi(p)\simeq\frac{m}{m+n}\Pi(p)\simeq\alpha\Pi(p)
\end{align*}
for $\delta$ close to 1.
before_annotation.question:
Consider the infinitely repeated (horizon $T=+\infty$) two-firm Bertrand price game (a supergame) in which the two firms produce perfect substitutes with the same marginal cost $c$. In each period $t=0,1,2,\ldots$, firms simultaneously choose prices $(p_{1t},p_{2t})$. Let $D(p)$ denote market demand at price $p$, and define
\[
\Pi(p)\equiv (p-c)D(p).
\]
If $p_{it}<p_{jt}$, firm $i$ gets the whole market and earns per-period profit $\Pi(p_{it})$; if $p_{it}=p_{jt}$, the firms share the market and firm $i$ earns $\Pi(p_{it})/2$; if $p_{it}>p_{jt}$, firm $i$ earns $0$. Let $p^m$ denote the monopoly price (it maximizes $(p-c)D(p)$), and let $\Pi^m\equiv \Pi(p^m)$.

A (history-dependent) strategy for firm $i$ specifies $p_{it}(H_t)$ after every history
\[
H_t \equiv (p_{10},p_{20};\ldots;p_{1,t-1},p_{2,t-1}).
\]
Firms discount future profits with factor $\delta\in(0,1)$. For a strategy profile, define firm $i$'s associated per-period payoff as
\[
(1-\delta)\sum_{t=0}^{\infty}\delta^t \Pi^i(p_{it},p_{jt}),
\]
and call a strategy profile a \emph{perfect equilibrium} if, for any history $H_t$ at date $t$, firm $i$'s continuation strategy from date $t$ on maximizes the present discounted value of its profits given firm $j$'s continuation strategy from that date on.

Show that any payoff $(\Pi^1,\Pi^2)$ such that $\Pi^1>0$, $\Pi^2>0$, and $\Pi^1+\Pi^2\le \Pi^m$ is an equilibrium payoff for $\delta$ close to $1$.

In each period $t=0,1,2,\ldots$, firms simultaneously choose prices $(p_{1t},p_{2t})$. Let $D(p)$ denote market demand at price $p$, and define
\[

\]
If $p_{it}<p_{jt}$, firm $i$ gets the whole market and earns per-period profit $\Pi(p_{it})$; if $p_{it}=p_{jt}$, the firms share the market and firm $i$ earns $\Pi(p_{it})/2$; if $p_{it}>p_{jt}$, firm $i$ earns $0$. Let $p^m$ denote the monopoly price (it maximizes $(p-c)D(p)$), and let $\Pi^m\equiv \Pi(p^m)$.

A (history-dependent) strategy for firm $i$ specifies $p_{it}(H_t)$ after every history
\[
H_t \equiv (p_{10},p_{20};\ldots;p_{1,t-1},p_{2,t-1}).
\]
Firms discount future profits with factor $\delta\in(0,1)$. For a strategy profile, define firm $i$'s associated per-period payoff as
\[
(1-\delta)\sum_{t=0}^{\infty}\delta^t \Pi^i(p_{it},p_{jt}),
\]
and call a strategy profile a \emph{perfect equilibrium} if, for any history $H_t$ at date $t$, firm $i$'s continuation strategy from date $t$ on maximizes the present discounted value of its profits given firm $j$'s continuation strategy from that date on.

In the construction described in the reference answer, along the cooperation path there is a cycle of length $m+n$ in which firm 1 gets the whole market at price $p$ for $m$ consecutive periods and firm 2 gets the whole market at price $p$ for $n$ consecutive periods, and this cycle repeats forever.

Question: What is firm 1's normalized discounted payoff on the cooperation path, $V_1^C$, as a function of $\delta$, $m$, $n$, and $\Pi_p$ (where $\Pi_p$ denotes $\Pi(p)$)?

Symbols (for final answer): $\delta, m, n, \Pi_p$.

Symbols (for final answer): - `Pi_p`: per-period monopoly profit at price p, i.e., Pi(p) - `delta`: discount factor in (0,1) - `m`: positive integer number of consecutive periods firm 1 gets the market in each cycle - `n`: positive integer number of consecutive periods firm 2 gets the market in each cycle
effective_after_annotation.reference_reasoning: On the cooperation path, firm 1 earns per-period profit \Pi_p in periods t=0,1,...,m-1 of each cycle of length m+n, and earns 0 in the remaining n periods of the cycle. The normalized discounted payoff is V_1^C=(1-\delta)\sum_{k\ge 0}\sum_{s=0}^{m-1} \delta^{k(m+n)+s} \Pi_p. Compute the inner sum: \sum_{s=0}^{m-1}\delta^s=(1-\delta^m)/(1-\delta). Then V_1^C=(1-\delta)\Pi_p \sum_{k\ge 0} \delta^{k(m+n)} \cdot (1-\delta^m)/(1-\delta) =\Pi_p(1-\delta^m)\sum_{k\ge 0}(\delta^{m+n})^k =\Pi_p(1-\delta^m)\cdot 1/(1-\delta^{m+n}).

question_standalone: Consider the infinitely repeated (horizon $T=+\infty$) two-firm Bertrand price game (a supergame) in which the two firms produce perfect substitutes with the same marginal cost $c$. In each period $t=0,1,2,\ldots$, firms simultaneously choose prices $(p_{1t},p_{2t})$. Let $D(p)$ denote market demand at price $p$, and define \[ \Pi(p)\equiv (p-c)D(p). \] If $p_{it}<p_{jt}$, firm $i$ gets the whole market and earns per-period profit $\Pi(p_{it})$; if $p_{it}=p_{jt}$, the firms share the market and firm $i$ earns $\Pi(p_{it})/2$; if $p_{it}>p_{jt}$, firm $i$ earns $0$. Symbols (for final answer): - `Pi_p`: per-period monopoly profit at price p, i.e., Pi(p) - `delta`: discount factor in (0,1) - `m`: positive integer number of consecutive periods firm 1 gets the market in each cycle - `n`: positive integer number of consecutive periods firm 2 gets the market in each cycle
reference_answer_corrected: Fix any target payoffs (\Pi^1,\Pi^2) with \Pi^1>0, \Pi^2>0 and \Pi^1+\Pi^2\le \Pi^m. Pick p\in[c,p^m] such that \Pi(p)=\Pi^1+\Pi^2 (possible since \Pi(c)=0 and \Pi(p^m)=\Pi^m and \Pi is continuous). Let \alpha\equiv \Pi^1/\Pi(p)\in(0,1). Choose integers m,n\ge1 so that m/(m+n) is arbitrarily close to \alpha. Consider the following (public) strategy profile. Cooperation path (cycle of length m+n): in periods t=0,1,\dots,m-1 firm 1 sets p and firm 2 sets some \bar p>p (so firm 1 serves the whole market at price p); in periods t=m,\dots,m+n-1 firm 2 sets p and firm 1 sets \bar p>p; then repeat this m+n cycle forever. Punishment: after any history in which the prescribed prices are not played (i.e., any deviation), both firms set p=c forever. In any punishment subgame, pricing at c is a one-shot Bertrand Nash equilibrium, so the continuation is optimal. On the cooperation path, in a period where firm i is supposed to get the market at price p, its prescribed action yields current profit \Pi(p) and continuation value V_i^C. A profitable one-shot deviation is to undercut slightly to p-\varepsilon, which yields at most \Pi^m in that period and triggers punishment thereafter (zero continuation). Thus no deviation is profitable if \[\Pi(p)+\delta V_i^C \ge \Pi^m.\] Since along the cooperation path firm i receives \Pi(p) in exactly m (for i=1) or n (for i=2) out of each m+n periods, its normalized discounted payoff is \[V_1^C=(1-\delta)\Pi(p)\sum_{k\ge0}\sum_{s=0}^{m-1}\delta^{k(m+n)+s}=\Pi(p)\frac{1-\delta^m}{1-\delta^{m+n}},\] \[V_2^C=\Pi(p)\frac{\delta^m(1-\delta^n)}{1-\delta^{m+n}}.\] As \delta\to1, V_1^C\to \frac{m}{m+n}\Pi(p) and V_2^C\to \frac{n}{m+n}\Pi(p). Also, as \delta\to1, \Pi(p)+\delta V_i^C\to \Pi(p)+\frac{m}{m+n}\Pi(p) (or \Pi(p)+\frac{n}{m+n}\Pi(p)), which is strictly positive; moreover, because V_i^C is bounded and increases to its limit, there exists \bar\delta<1 such that for all \delta\in(\bar\delta,1) the incentive constraint \Pi(p)+\delta V_i^C\ge \Pi^m holds for both firms (since the gain from deviation is bounded above by \Pi^m while the loss from triggering punishment approaches the full continuation value as \delta\to1). Therefore, for \delta sufficiently close to 1, the above strategies form a perfect equilibrium. The induced payoffs satisfy V_1^C\approx \frac{m}{m+n}\Pi(p)\approx \alpha\Pi(p)=\Pi^1 and V_2^C\approx \Pi^2. Hence any (\Pi^1,\Pi^2) with \Pi^1>0, \Pi^2>0, \Pi^1+\Pi^2\le \Pi^m is an equilibrium payoff for \delta close to 1.
reference_answer_latex_from_by_problem:
We content ourselves with showing that any payoff $(\Pi^1,\Pi^2)$ can be approximated as closely as desired for $\delta$ close to 1. Choose a price $p$ in $[c,p^m]$ such that $\Pi(p)=\Pi^1+\Pi^2$, and let $\Pi^1=\alpha\Pi(p)$ and $\Pi^2=(1-\alpha)\Pi(p)$.

Consider the ratio $\alpha/(1-\alpha)$. We know that any real number can be approximated as closely as is wished by a rational number. Let $m/n$ denote a rational approximation of $\alpha/(1-\alpha)$. Consider the following strategies: ``During the first $m$ periods, firm 1 charges $p$ and firm 2 charges a price strictly exceeding $p$; for the $n$ subsequent periods, firm 2 charges $p$ and firm 1 charges a price strictly exceeding $p$; during the $m$ subsequent periods, it is firm 1's turn to have the market at price $p$; and so forth. If anyone deviates, the firms charge the marginal cost for ever.'' These strategies clearly form an equilibrium for $\delta$ close to 1. Furthermore, the per-period payoff for firm 1 is
\begin{align*}
(1-\delta)\Pi(p)\big[(1+\delta+\cdots+\delta^{m-1})+(\delta^{m+n}+\cdots+\delta^{2m+n-1})+\cdots\big]\\
=\frac{1+\delta+\cdots+\delta^{m-1}}{1+\delta+\cdots+\delta^{m+n-1}}\Pi(p)\simeq\frac{m}{m+n}\Pi(p)\simeq\alpha\Pi(p)
\end{align*}
for $\delta$ close to 1.

step5.question: Consider the infinitely repeated (horizon $T=+\infty$) two-firm Bertrand price game (a supergame) in which the two firms produce perfect substitutes with the same marginal cost $c$. In each period $t=0,1,2,\ldots$, firms simultaneously choose prices $(p_{1t},p_{2t})$. Let $D(p)$ denote market demand at price $p$, and define \[ \Pi(p)\equiv (p-c)D(p). \] If $p_{it}<p_{jt}$, firm $i$ gets the whole market and earns per-period profit $\Pi(p_{it})$; if $p_{it}=p_{jt}$, the firms share the market and firm $i$ earns $\Pi(p_{it})/2$; if $p_{it}>p_{jt}$, firm $i$ earns $0$. Let $p^m$ denote the monopoly price (it maximizes $(p-c)D(p)$), and let $\Pi^m\equiv \Pi(p^m)$. A (history-dependent) strategy for firm $i$ specifies $p_{it}(H_t)$ after every history \[ H_t \equiv (p_{10},p_{20};\ldots;p_{1,t-1},p_{2,t-1}). \] Firms discount future profits with factor $\delta\in(0,1)$. For a strategy profile, define firm $i$'s associated per-period payoff as \[ (1-\delta)\sum_{t=0}^{\infty}\delta^t \Pi^i(p_{it},p_{jt}), \] and call a strategy profile a \emph{perfect equilibrium} if, for any history $H_t$ at date $t$, firm $i$'s continuation strategy from date $t$ on maximizes the present discounted value of its profits given firm $j$'s continuation strategy from that date on. In the construction described in the reference answer, along the cooperation path there is a cycle of length $m+n$ in which firm 1 gets the whole market at price $p$ for $m$ consecutive periods and firm 2 gets the whole market at price $p$ for $n$ consecutive periods, and this cycle repeats forever. Question: What is firm 1's normalized discounted payoff on the cooperation path, $V_1^C$, as a function of `delta`, `m`, `n`, and `Pi_p` (where `Pi_p` denotes $\Pi(p)$)? Symbols (for final answer): - `Pi_p`: per-period monopoly profit at price p, i.e., Pi(p) - `delta`: discount factor in (0,1) - `m`: positive integer number of consecutive periods firm 1 gets the market in each cycle - `n`: positive integer number of consecutive periods firm 2 gets the market in each cycle
step5.reference_reasoning: On the cooperation path, firm 1 earns per-period profit \Pi_p in periods t=0,1,...,m-1 of each cycle of length m+n, and earns 0 in the remaining n periods of the cycle. The normalized discounted payoff is V_1^C=(1-\delta)\sum_{k\ge 0}\sum_{s=0}^{m-1} \delta^{k(m+n)+s} \Pi_p. Compute the inner sum: \sum_{s=0}^{m-1}\delta^s=(1-\delta^m)/(1-\delta). Then V_1^C=(1-\delta)\Pi_p \sum_{k\ge 0} \delta^{k(m+n)} \cdot (1-\delta^m)/(1-\delta) =\Pi_p(1-\delta^m)\sum_{k\ge 0}(\delta^{m+n})^k =\Pi_p(1-\delta^m)\cdot 1/(1-\delta^{m+n}).

symbol_contract_definitions.Pi_p: per-period monopoly profit at price p, i.e., Pi(p)
symbol_contract_definitions.m: positive integer number of consecutive periods firm 1 gets the market in each cycle
symbol_contract_definitions.n: positive integer number of consecutive periods firm 2 gets the market in each cycle

annotator_overrides.question:
Consider the (infinite-horizon) repeated price game between two firms producing perfect substitutes with the same constant marginal cost $c$. In each period $t=0,1,2,\dots$, firms simultaneously choose prices $(p_{1t},p_{2t})$. Let $D(p)$ denote market demand at price $p$, and define the profit from serving the whole market at price $p$ by
\[
\Pi(p)\equiv (p-c)D(p).
\]
If one firm charges a strictly lower price than its rival, it gets the whole market (so the higher-price firm sells nothing). If both firms charge the same price $p$, they share the market: firm 1 sells a share $s_1$ and firm 2 sells a share $1-s_1$, so their one-period profits are $s_1\Pi(p)$ and $(1-s_1)\Pi(p)$, respectively. A pure strategy for firm $i$ specifies, for each date $t$, a price $p_{it}$ as a function of the history of past prices
\[
H_t \equiv (p_{10},p_{20},\dots,p_{1,t-1},p_{2,t-1}).
\]
Each firm maximizes the discounted sum of one-period profits, and we associate to any strategy profile the (normalized) per-period profit
\[
(1-\delta)\sum_{t=0}^{\infty}\delta^t\,\Pi^i_t,
\]
where $\delta\in(0,1)$ is the discount factor and $\Pi^i_t$ is firm $i$'s profit in period $t$. A (subgame-)perfect equilibrium is a strategy profile such that after every history $H_t$, each firm's continuation strategy maximizes its continuation discounted payoff given the rival's continuation strategy.

Let $\mathrm{bar\_pi}$ be the supremum of (normalized) per-period profits that a firm can obtain in any pure-strategy subgame-perfect equilibrium. For $\delta\le 1/2$, compute $\mathrm{bar\_pi}$.

Symbols (for final answer):
- `bar_pi`: supremum of normalized per-period profits attainable by a firm in any pure-strategy subgame-perfect equilibrium
- `delta`: discount factor in (0,1)

before_annotation.answer:
The highest possible per-period profit, $\bar{\Pi}$, is the same for both firms (because the game is symmetric, the set of attainable per-period profits is also symmetric). Consider an equilibrium in which firm 1, say, gets a per-period profit $\bar{\Pi}-\varepsilon$ (where $\varepsilon$ is positive and small), and consider a price $p$ such that $\Pi(p)\ge \bar{\Pi}-\varepsilon$, price $p$ is the lowest price charged for some period $t$, and firm 1 makes profits $s_1\Pi(p)\ge \bar{\Pi}-\varepsilon$ in that period. (Such a price and period must exist; otherwise firm 1 could not make $\bar{\Pi}-\varepsilon$ ``on average.'') At $t$ firm 2 ought to deviate and charge slightly less than $p$. By doing so, it would gain $s_1\Pi(p)/2$ at date $t$ (because it would capture the whole market). But the loss of future collusion costs at most
\[
\bar{\Pi}(\delta+\delta^2+\cdots)=\bar{\Pi}\frac{\delta}{1-\delta}<\bar{\Pi}-\varepsilon.
\]
It suffices to choose $\varepsilon$ such that
\[
\frac{\bar{\Pi}-\varepsilon}{\bar{\Pi}}>\frac{\delta}{1-\delta}
\]
to obtain the contradiction.

effective_after_annotation.reference_reasoning: Let bar_pi be the supremum normalized per-period profit attainable by a firm in any pure-strategy SPE. Suppose bar_pi>0. Pick eps in (0,bar_pi) and an SPE where firm 1 gets at least bar_pi-eps. Then along the equilibrium path there is some period t with positive profit and a minimal such price p_t>c; at that t both firms must charge p_t, so firm 1 earns pi_1t=s_1*Pi(p_t)>0. Choose t so that pi_1t>=bar_pi-eps (otherwise the normalized average would be <bar_pi-eps). Consider firm 2 deviating at history H_t to p_t-eta with eta>0 small. Then firm 2 captures the whole market at t and its one-period profit approaches Pi(p_t) as eta->0, whereas its equilibrium one-period profit is (1-s_1)Pi(p_t). Thus the one-period gain from deviating can be made arbitrarily close to Pi(p_t)-(1-s_1)Pi(p_t)=s_1Pi(p_t)=pi_1t>=bar_pi-eps. The maximal continuation loss (in normalized value) from t+1 onward is bounded by the present value of losing at most bar_pi per period forever: (1-delta)*sum_{k=1}^infty delta^k*bar_pi = delta*bar_pi. For the deviation not to be profitable, we need bar_pi-eps <= delta*bar_pi. Letting eps->0 gives bar_pi <= delta*bar_pi, which with delta<1 implies bar_pi=0. Hence for all delta<=1/2, bar_pi=0.

reference_answer_corrected: Let \(\bar\pi\) be the supremum of (normalized) per-period profits that firm 1 can obtain in any pure-strategy subgame-perfect equilibrium (SPE). By symmetry the same supremum applies to firm 2. Assume toward a contradiction that \(\bar\pi>0\) for some \(\delta\le 1/2\). Fix \(\varepsilon\in(0,\bar\pi)\) and take a pure-strategy SPE in which firm 1’s normalized per-period profit is at least \(\bar\pi-\varepsilon\). In that equilibrium path, there must exist some date \(t\) at which the market price is \(p_t>c\) and firm 1 earns strictly positive one-period profit. Let \(t\) be a date at which the market price is minimal among all dates on the equilibrium path with positive profit for firm 1 (so in particular, at date \(t\) the two firms must be charging the same price \(p_t\); otherwise the high-price firm earns 0). Then firm 1’s one-period profit at \(t\) is \[ \pi_{1t}=s_1\Pi(p_t)>0. \] Moreover, since firm 1’s normalized average profit is at least \(\bar\pi-\varepsilon\), we can choose such a \(t\) with \[ \pi_{1t}\ge \bar\pi-\varepsilon. \] (Otherwise all positive-profit periods would have \(\pi_{1t}<\bar\pi-\varepsilon\), implying the normalized average is < \(\bar\pi-\varepsilon\).) Consider now firm 2’s deviation after history \(H_t\): set \(p_{2t}=p_t-\eta\) for small \(\eta>0\). Then firm 2 captures the whole market at date \(t\) and earns approximately \[ \pi'_{2t}=\Pi(p_t-\eta)\to \Pi(p_t)\quad (\eta\downarrow 0). \] Since at \(t\) the equilibrium has both firms pricing at \(p_t\), firm 2’s equilibrium one-period profit is \((1-s_1)\Pi(p_t)\). Thus the one-period gain from deviating can be made arbitrarily close to \[ \Pi(p_t)-(1-s_1)\Pi(p_t)=s_1\Pi(p_t)=\pi_{1t}\ge \bar\pi-\varepsilon. \] What can firm 2 lose in continuation value from triggering any punishment? In any continuation subgame, firm 2’s normalized per-period equilibrium profit is at most \(\bar\pi\) by definition of \(\bar\pi\). Hence the maximal possible loss in normalized value from date \(t+1\) onward is bounded by the present value of receiving \(\bar\pi\) forever versus 0 forever: \[ (1-\delta)\sum_{k=1}^\infty \delta^k\bar\pi=\delta\bar\pi. \] Therefore, for the deviation not to be profitable we must have \[ \bar\pi-\varepsilon\le \delta\bar\pi. \] Letting \(\varepsilon\downarrow 0\) yields \(\bar\pi\le \delta\bar\pi\), so if \(\delta<1\) this implies \(\bar\pi=0\). In particular for all \(\delta\le 1/2\), \(\bar\pi=0\). Thus in any pure-strategy SPE each firm’s normalized per-period profit is 0. Since profits are nonnegative only when price \(>c\), this implies that on the equilibrium path the market price must be \(c\) in every period (otherwise some firm would earn positive profit), i.e. the only equilibrium profit is the competitive (zero) profit.

A (subgame-)perfect equilibrium is a strategy profile such that after every history $H_t$, each firm's continuation strategy maximizes its continuation discounted payoff given the rival's continuation strategy.

Show that for $\delta \leq \frac{1}{2}$ the only equilibrium profit is the competitive (zero) profit. Restrict yourself to pure strategies. Hint: Consider the highest (supremum) per-period profit that is attainable by a firm in a perfect equilibrium.
split_answer: Let \(\bar\pi\) be the supremum of (normalized) per-period profits that firm 1 can obtain in any pure-strategy subgame-perfect equilibrium (SPE). By symmetry the same supremum applies to firm 2. Assume toward a contradiction that \(\bar\pi>0\) for some \(\delta\le 1/2\). Fix \(\varepsilon\in(0,\bar\pi)\) and take a pure-strategy SPE in which firm 1’s normalized per-period profit is at least \(\bar\pi-\varepsilon\). In that equilibrium path, there must exist some date \(t\) at which the market price is \(p_t>c\) and firm 1 earns strictly positive one-period profit. Let \(t\) be a date at which the market price is minimal among all dates on the equilibrium path with positive profit for firm 1 (so in particular, at date \(t\) the two firms must be charging the same price \(p_t\); otherwise the high-price firm earns 0). Then firm 1’s one-period profit at \(t\) is \[ \pi_{1t}=s_1\Pi(p_t)>0. \] Moreover, since firm 1’s normalized average profit is at least \(\bar\pi-\varepsilon\), we can choose such a \(t\) with \[ \pi_{1t}\ge \bar\pi-\varepsilon. \] (Otherwise all positive-profit periods would have \(\pi_{1t}<\bar\pi-\varepsilon\), implying the normalized average is < \(\bar\pi-\varepsilon\).) Consider now firm 2’s deviation after history \(H_t\): set \(p_{2t}=p_t-\eta\) for small \(\eta>0\). Then firm 2 captures the whole market at date \(t\) and earns approximately \[ \pi'_{2t}=\Pi(p_t-\eta)\to \Pi(p_t)\quad (\eta\downarrow 0). \] Since at \(t\) the equilibrium has both firms pricing at \(p_t\), firm 2’s equilibrium one-period profit is \((1-s_1)\Pi(p_t)\). Thus the one-period gain from deviating can be made arbitrarily close to \[ \Pi(p_t)-(1-s_1)\Pi(p_t)=s_1\Pi(p_t)=\pi_{1t}\ge \bar\pi-\varepsilon. \] What can firm 2 lose in continuation value from triggering any punishment? In any continuation subgame, firm 2’s normalized per-period equilibrium profit is at most \(\bar\pi\) by definition of \(\bar\pi\). Hence the maximal possible loss in normalized value from date \(t+1\) onward is bounded by the present value of receiving \(\bar\pi\) forever versus 0 forever: \[ (1-\delta)\sum_{k=1}^\infty \delta^k\bar\pi=\delta\bar\pi. \] Therefore, for the deviation not to be profitable we must have \[ \bar\pi-\varepsilon\le \delta\bar\pi. \] Letting \(\varepsilon\downarrow 0\) yields \(\bar\pi\le \delta\bar\pi\), so if \(\delta<1\) this implies \(\bar\pi=0\). In particular for all \(\delta\le 1/2\), \(\bar\pi=0\). Thus in any pure-strategy SPE each firm’s normalized per-period profit is 0. Since profits are nonnegative only when price \(>c\), this implies that on the equilibrium path the market price must be \(c\) in every period (otherwise some firm would earn positive profit), i.e. the only equilibrium profit is the competitive (zero) profit.
split_question:
Consider the (infinite-horizon) repeated price game between two firms producing perfect substitutes with the same constant marginal cost $c$. In each period $t=0,1,2,\dots$, firms simultaneously choose prices $(p_{1t},p_{2t})$. Let $D(p)$ denote market demand at price $p$, and define the profit from serving the whole market at price $p$ by
\[
\Pi(p)\equiv (p-c)D(p).
\]
If one firm charges a strictly lower price than its rival, it gets the whole market (so the higher-price firm sells nothing). If both firms charge the same price $p$, they share the market: firm 1 sells a share $s_1$ and firm 2 sells a share $1-s_1$, so their one-period profits are $s_1\Pi(p)$ and $(1-s_1)\Pi(p)$, respectively. A pure strategy for firm $i$ specifies, for each date $t$, a price $p_{it}$ as a function of the history of past prices
\[
H_t \equiv (p_{10},p_{20},\dots,p_{1,t-1},p_{2,t-1}).
\]
Each firm maximizes the discounted sum of one-period profits, and we associate to any strategy profile the (normalized) per-period profit
\[
(1-\delta)\sum_{t=0}^{\infty}\delta^t\,\Pi^i_t,
\]
where $\delta\in(0,1)$ is the discount factor and $\Pi^i_t$ is firm $i$'s profit in period $t$. A (subgame-)perfect equilibrium is a strategy profile such that after every history $H_t$, each firm's continuation strategy maximizes its continuation discounted payoff given the rival's continuation strategy.

Show that for $\delta \leq \frac{1}{2}$ the only equilibrium profit is the competitive (zero) profit. Restrict yourself to pure strategies. Hint: Consider the highest (supremum) per-period profit that is attainable by a firm in a perfect equilibrium.

Let $D(p)$ denote market demand at price $p$, and define the profit from serving the whole market at price $p$ by \[ \Pi(p)\equiv (p-c)D(p). \] If one firm charges a strictly lower price than its rival, it gets the whole market (so the higher-price firm sells nothing). If both firms charge the same price $p$, they share the market: firm 1 sells a share $s_1$ and firm 2 sells a share $1-s_1$, so their one-period profits are $s_1\Pi(p)$ and $(1-s_1)\Pi(p)$, respectively. A pure strategy for firm $i$ specifies, for each date $t$, a price $p_{it}$ as a function of the history of past prices \[ H_t \equiv (p_{10},p_{20},\dots,p_{1,t-1},p_{2,t-1}). \] Each firm maximizes the discounted sum of one-period profits, and we associate to any strategy profile the (normalized) per-period profit \[ (1-\delta)\sum_{t=0}^{\infty}\delta^t\,\Pi^i_t, \] where $\delta\in(0,1)$ is the discount factor and $\Pi^i_t$ is firm $i$'s profit in period $t$. A (subgame-)perfect equilibrium is a strategy profile such that after every history $H_t$, each firm's continuation strategy maximizes its continuation discounted payoff given the rival's continuation strategy. Let $\mathrm{bar\_pi}$ be the supremum of (normalized) per-period profits that a firm can obtain in any pure-strategy subgame-perfect equilibrium. For $\delta\le 1/2$, compute $\mathrm{bar\_pi}$. Symbols (for final answer): - `bar_pi`: supremum of normalized per-period profits attainable by a firm in any pure-strategy subgame-perfect equilibrium - `delta`: discount factor in (0,1)
step5.reference_reasoning: Let bar_pi be the supremum normalized per-period profit attainable by a firm in any pure-strategy SPE. Suppose bar_pi>0. Pick eps in (0,bar_pi) and an SPE where firm 1 gets at least bar_pi-eps. Then along the equilibrium path there is some period t with positive profit and a minimal such price p_t>c; at that t both firms must charge p_t, so firm 1 earns pi_1t=s_1*Pi(p_t)>0. Choose t so that pi_1t>=bar_pi-eps (otherwise the normalized average would be <bar_pi-eps). Consider firm 2 deviating at history H_t to p_t-eta with eta>0 small. Then firm 2 captures the whole market at t and its one-period profit approaches Pi(p_t) as eta->0, whereas its equilibrium one-period profit is (1-s_1)Pi(p_t). Thus the one-period gain from deviating can be made arbitrarily close to Pi(p_t)-(1-s_1)Pi(p_t)=s_1Pi(p_t)=pi_1t>=bar_pi-eps. The maximal continuation loss (in normalized value) from t+1 onward is bounded by the present value of losing at most bar_pi per period forever: (1-delta)*sum_{k=1}^infty delta^k*bar_pi = delta*bar_pi. For the deviation not to be profitable, we need bar_pi-eps <= delta*bar_pi. Letting eps->0 gives bar_pi <= delta*bar_pi, which with delta<1 implies bar_pi=0. Hence for all delta<=1/2, bar_pi=0.

before_annotation.answer:
The monopoly price is sustainable if
\[
\left(\frac{n-1}{n}\right)\Pi^m\le \frac{\Pi^m}{n}\bigl(\delta\mu+\delta^2\mu^2+\cdots\bigr).
\]
(The LHS is the gain from deviating; the RHS is the long-term loss.) That is,
\[
\delta\mu\ge 1-\frac{1}{n}.
\]
For a given $\delta$, this condition is more easily satisfied if the market is expanding. (The intuition is that the future weighs more heavily in such circumstances.)
before_annotation.question: Consider an $n$-firm supergame framework. The firms have constant marginal cost $c$. The demand function at date $t$ is $q_t=\mu^tD(p_t)$, where $\mu\delta<1$ ($\delta$ is the discount factor). Derive the set of discount factors such that full collusion (i.e., the monopoly solution) is sustainable as an equilibrium of the supergame. What would this model predict about the relative ease of sustaining collusion in expanding and declining industries?

effective_after_annotation.question: Consider an $n$-firm supergame framework. The firms have constant marginal cost $c$. The demand function at date $t$ is $q_t=\mu^tD(p_t)$, where $\mu\delta<1$ ($\delta$ is the discount factor). Derive the set of discount factors such that full collusion (i.e., the monopoly solution) is sustainable as an equilibrium of the supergame. Compute the minimum discount factor $\delta_{\min}$ (as a function of $n$ and $\mu$) such that full collusion is sustainable under grim-trigger reversion to the competitive outcome. Symbols (for final answer): - `mu`: demand growth/decay factor (mu) - `n`: number of firms
effective_after_annotation.reference_reasoning: Let pi_m be the one-period monopoly profit when demand is q=D(p) at t=0. At date t, monopoly profit is mu^t*pi_m. Under symmetric full collusion each firm gets mu^t*pi_m/n each period. If a firm deviates in period t while others charge the monopoly price, it can capture the whole monopoly profit in that period, i.e. mu^t*pi_m. With grim-trigger reversion to the competitive outcome (zero profit) thereafter, the incentive constraint at any t is: sum_{k=0}^infty (delta*mu)^k * (pi_m/n) >= pi_m. This gives (pi_m/n) * 1/(1-delta*mu) >= pi_m, so 1/(n(1-delta*mu)) >= 1, hence delta*mu >= 1 - 1/n. Therefore the minimum discount factor is delta_min = (1 - 1/n)/mu.

question_standalone: Consider an $n$-firm supergame framework. The firms have constant marginal cost $c$. The demand function at date $t$ is $q_t=\mu^tD(p_t)$, where $\mu\delta<1$ ($\delta$ is the discount factor). Derive the set of discount factors such that full collusion (i.e., the monopoly solution) is sustainable as an equilibrium of the supergame. Compute the minimum discount factor $\delta_{\min}$ (as a function of $n$ and $\mu$) such that full collusion is sustainable under grim-trigger reversion to the competitive outcome. Symbols (for final answer): - `mu`: demand growth/decay factor (mu) - `n`: number of firms
reference_answer_corrected: Let \(\pi^m\) denote the one-period monopoly profit when demand is \(q=D(p)\) (i.e., at \(t=0\)). At date \(t\), monopoly profit is \(\mu^t\pi^m\). Under symmetric full collusion each firm gets \(\mu^t\pi^m/n\) each period. If a firm deviates in period \(t\) while others charge the monopoly price, it can capture the whole monopoly profit in that period, i.e. \(\mu^t\pi^m\). With grim-trigger reversion to the competitive outcome (zero profit) thereafter, the incentive constraint at any \(t\) is \[ \sum_{k=0}^\infty (\delta\mu)^k\,\frac{\pi^m}{n}\;\ge\;\pi^m. \] Equivalently, \[ \frac{\pi^m}{n}\cdot \frac{1}{1-\delta\mu}\ge \pi^m \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad \delta\mu\ge 1-\frac{1}{n} \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad \delta\ge \frac{1-1/n}{\mu}. \] Thus full collusion is sustainable iff \(\delta\mu\ge 1-1/n\). Prediction: collusion is easier to sustain in expanding industries (\(\mu>1\)) and harder in declining industries (\(\mu<1\)), because growth raises the effective discount factor \(\delta\mu\).

annotator_overrides.question:
Consider two firms interacting in two identical and independent markets. In each market, the firms would like to sustain a constant collusive price (e.g., the monopoly price). Let $\Pi^m$ denote the per-period industry profit associated with charging the monopoly price in a market. Under full collusion, each firm then receives $\Pi^m/2$ per market per period. A unilateral undercut of the monopoly price in a market yields a one-period gain of $\Pi^m/2$ in that market (relative to collusion). After any detected deviation, firms revert forever to Bertrand behavior (the maximal punishment), yielding zero profit in the punished market(s). The common discount factor between periods is $\delta\in(0,1)$.

The markets differ in that in market 1 a firm's price at time $t$ is observed at $t+1$, whereas in market 2 it is learned only at $t+2$. Thus, although each of the markets meets every period, market 2 has longer information lags.

In the absence of multimarket contact, what is the minimum discount factor $\delta$ (as an exact expression) such that collusion in market 2 is sustainable?

Symbols (for final answer):
- delta: discount factor

before_annotation.answer: (i) The implicit discount factor in market 2 is $\delta^2$---a firm can deviate during two consecutive periods without being detected.

The markets differ in that in market 1 a firm's price at time $t$ is observed at $t+1$, whereas in market 2 it is learned only at $t+2$. Thus, although each of the markets meets every period, market 2 has longer information lags.

(i) Argue that in the absence of multimarket contact, collusion in market 2 would be sustainable if and only if $\delta \ge 1/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.71$.

effective_after_annotation.reference_reasoning: In market 2, a deviation at time t is detected only at t+2, so the deviator can obtain the deviation gain for two periods before punishment starts. If a firm colludes forever, its PV from market 2 is V_C = (Pi^m/2)/(1-delta). If it deviates at t and continues to undercut at t+1 (still undetected), it gets an extra gain Pi^m/2 in each of the two periods, and from t+2 onward gets 0 in that market, so V_D = (Pi^m/2)/(1-delta) + (Pi^m/2) + delta*(Pi^m/2). Sustainability requires V_C >= V_D, which reduces to delta^2 >= 1/2, hence delta >= 1/sqrt(2). Therefore the minimum discount factor is 1/sqrt(2).

If a firm sticks to collusion, its present value from market 2 is \[ V_C=\frac{\Pi^m/2}{1-\delta}. \] If it deviates (undercuts) at t and continues to undercut at t+1 (still undetected), it gets an extra gain of \(\Pi^m/2\) in each of the two periods, and from t+2 onward gets 0 in that market: \[ V_D=\frac{\Pi^m/2}{1-\delta}+\frac{\Pi^m}{2}+\delta\,\frac{\Pi^m}{2}. \] Collusion is sustainable iff \(V_C\ge V_D\), i.e. \[ \frac{\Pi^m/2}{1-\delta}\ge \frac{\Pi^m/2}{1-\delta}+\frac{\Pi^m}{2}(1+\delta) \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad \frac{\delta^2}{1-\delta^2}\,\frac{\Pi^m}{2}\ge \frac{\Pi^m}{2}(1+\delta) \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad \delta^2\ge \tfrac12. \] Thus collusion in market 2 is sustainable iff \(\delta\ge 1/\sqrt{2}\).

split_question:
Consider two firms interacting in two identical and independent markets. In each market, the firms would like to sustain a constant collusive price (e.g., the monopoly price). Let $\Pi^m$ denote the per-period industry profit associated with charging the monopoly price in a market. Under full collusion, each firm then receives $\Pi^m/2$ per market per period. A unilateral undercut of the monopoly price in a market yields a one-period gain of $\Pi^m/2$ in that market (relative to collusion). After any detected deviation, firms revert forever to Bertrand behavior (the maximal punishment), yielding zero profit in the punished market(s). The common discount factor between periods is $\delta\in(0,1)$.

The markets differ in that in market 1 a firm's price at time $t$ is observed at $t+1$, whereas in market 2 it is learned only at $t+2$. Thus, although each of the markets meets every period, market 2 has longer information lags.

(i) Argue that in the absence of multimarket contact, collusion in market 2 would be sustainable if and only if $\delta \ge 1/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.71$.

effective_after_annotation.reference_reasoning: Under multimarket contact, any detected deviation in either market triggers reversion to Bertrand in both markets forever. The optimal deviation exploits the longer information lag in market 2: deviate in market 2 at time t (not observed until t+2), gaining an extra Pi^m/2 at t. At time t+1, before punishment can start, deviate in both markets (market 1 deviation observed at t+2, and deviate again in market 2), gaining an extra Pi^m/2 in each market at t+1. Thus deviation gain is G = (Pi^m/2) + delta*(Pi^m/2 + Pi^m/2) = (Pi^m/2)(1+2 delta). Punishment starts at t+2, so the deviator loses collusive profits in both markets from t+2 onward. Under collusion it earns Pi^m per period total, so discounted loss is L = sum_{k=2}^infty delta^k Pi^m = (delta^2/(1-delta)) Pi^m. Sustainability requires G <= L: (Pi^m/2)(1+2 delta) <= (delta^2/(1-delta)) Pi^m. Cancel Pi^m and rearrange to 1 + delta - 4 delta^2 <= 0. The binding threshold is the larger root of 4 delta^2 - delta - 1 = 0, giving delta_bar = (1 + sqrt(17))/8 ≈ 0.6404.

reference_answer_corrected: Under multimarket contact, a deviation in either market triggers (once detected) reversion to Bertrand in both markets forever. If a firm deviates optimally, it should: - Deviate in market 2 at time t (not observed until t+2), gaining an extra \(\Pi^m/2\) at t. - At time t+1, before any punishment can start, deviate in both markets: in market 1 (observed at t+2) and again in market 2, gaining an extra \(\Pi^m/2\) in each market at t+1. Thus the discounted one-shot deviation gain is \[ G=\frac{\Pi^m}{2}+\delta\left(\frac{\Pi^m}{2}+\frac{\Pi^m}{2}\right)=\frac{\Pi^m}{2}(1+2\delta). \] Punishment starts at t+2, so the deviator forgoes collusive profits in both markets from t+2 onward. Under collusion it would earn \(\Pi^m\) per period total (\(\Pi^m/2\) per market), so the discounted loss is \[ L=\sum_{k=2}^\infty \delta^k \Pi^m=\frac{\delta^2}{1-\delta}\,\Pi^m. \] Collusion is sustainable iff \(G\le L\): \[ \frac{\Pi^m}{2}(1+2\delta)\le \frac{\delta^2}{1-\delta}\Pi^m \iff 1+\delta-4\delta^2\le 0 \iff \delta\ge \bar\delta=\frac{1+\sqrt{17}}{8}\approx 0.6404. \] Hence collusion in both markets is sustainable iff \(\delta\ge \bar\delta\approx 0.64\).

before_annotation.answer:
See section 6.7. For $\alpha=\frac{1}{4}$, $T$ is the lowest time such that
\[
3\delta-\delta^{T+1}\ge 2.
\]
Hence, $\delta\ge \frac{2}{3}$ if this condition is to be satisfied for some $T$. For $T=1$, it is not satisfied unless $\delta=1$.

effective_after_annotation.question: Work your way through the Green-Porter model with price competition and two i.i.d.\ states of demand (no demand or positive demand). Rederive the optimal length of punishment. Show that for a probability $\alpha = \frac{1}{4}$ of no demand, $\delta$ must exceed $\frac{2}{3}$ in order for collusion to be sustainable; show also that at least two periods of punishment are required. Consider the following infinite-horizon supergame. Two firms choose prices each period. The goods are perfect substitutes and are produced at constant marginal cost $c$, so consumers all buy from the low-price firm; if both firms charge the same price, demand is split equally. In each period there are two possible states of demand: with probability $\alpha$ there is no demand (the ``low-demand state''), and with probability $1-\alpha$ there is positive demand $D(p)$ (the ``high-demand state''). Demand realizations are i.i.d.\ over time. Let $p^m$ denote the monopoly price for unit cost $c$ (i.e.\ $p^m$ maximizes $(p-c)D(p)$), and let $\Pi^m$ denote the monopoly profit in the high-demand state. Firms do not observe rivals' prices directly, but it is always common knowledge that at least one firm makes no profit in each period. A firm that does not sell cannot tell whether this is due to the low-demand state or to its rival having charged a lower price. Analyze the following (pure) strategies, which feature a collusive phase and a punishment phase. The game begins in the collusive phase. In the collusive phase, both firms charge $p^m$ until a period in which at least one firm makes zero profit; the occurrence of a zero-profit event is observed by both firms and triggers a punishment phase. In the punishment phase, both firms charge the competitive (Bertrand) price $c$ for exactly $T$ periods (where $T$ is a nonnegative integer, possibly $+\infty$), and then revert to the collusive phase. Let $V^+$ (respectively, $V^-$) denote the present discounted value of a firm's profits from some date $t$ onward, assuming that at date $t$ the game is in the collusive phase (respectively, starts the punishment phase). By stationarity, $V^+$ and $V^-$ do not depend on time. By definition, these values satisfy \begin{align} V^+ &= (1-\alpha)\Bigl(\Pi^m/2 + \delta V^+\Bigr) + \alpha\bigl(\delta V^-\bigr), \\ V^- &= \delta^T V^+. \end{align} In the collusive phase, an undercutting deviation (charging below $p^m$) yields current profit $\Pi^m$ if demand is high and triggers the punishment phase next period; this gives the incentive constraint \begin{equation} \delta\bigl(V^+ - V^-\bigr) \ge \Pi^m/2. \end{equation} Equations imply the sustainability condition \begin{equation} 1 \le 2(1-\alpha)\delta + (2\alpha-1)\delta^{T+1}. \end{equation} For $\alpha=\tfrac14$, compute the threshold value of $\delta$ (as a number) such that collusion can be sustainable for some (possibly infinite) $T$. Symbols (for final answer): - delta_threshold: the threshold value of $\delta$
effective_after_annotation.reference_reasoning: With \alpha=1/4, the condition becomes 1 \le (3/2)\delta - (1/2)\delta^{T+1}, i.e. 3\delta-\delta^{T+1}\ge 2. For a given \delta\in[0,1), the left-hand side is increasing in T and its supremum over T is \lim_{T\to\infty}(3\delta-\delta^{T+1})=3\delta. Thus existence of some (possibly infinite) T satisfying the inequality requires 3\delta\ge 2, i.e. \delta\ge 2/3. Therefore the threshold is 2/3.

Demand realizations are i.i.d.\ over time. Let $p^m$ denote the monopoly price for unit cost $c$ (i.e.\ $p^m$ maximizes $(p-c)D(p)$), and let $\Pi^m$ denote the monopoly profit in the high-demand state. Firms do not observe rivals' prices directly, but it is always common knowledge that at least one firm makes no profit in each period. A firm that does not sell cannot tell whether this is due to the low-demand state or to its rival having charged a lower price. Analyze the following (pure) strategies, which feature a collusive phase and a punishment phase. The game begins in the collusive phase. In the collusive phase, both firms charge $p^m$ until a period in which at least one firm makes zero profit; the occurrence of a zero-profit event is observed by both firms and triggers a punishment phase. In the punishment phase, both firms charge the competitive (Bertrand) price $c$ for exactly $T$ periods (where $T$ is a nonnegative integer, possibly $+\infty$), and then revert to the collusive phase. Let $V^+$ (respectively, $V^-$) denote the present discounted value of a firm's profits from some date $t$ onward, assuming that at date $t$ the game is in the collusive phase (respectively, starts the punishment phase). By stationarity, $V^+$ and $V^-$ do not depend on time. By definition, these values satisfy \begin{align} V^+ &= (1-\alpha)\Bigl(\Pi^m/2 + \delta V^+\Bigr) + \alpha\bigl(\delta V^-\bigr), \\ V^- &= \delta^T V^+. \end{align} In the collusive phase, an undercutting deviation (charging below $p^m$) yields current profit $\Pi^m$ if demand is high and triggers the punishment phase next period; this gives the incentive constraint \begin{equation} \delta\bigl(V^+ - V^-\bigr) \ge \Pi^m/2. \end{equation} Equations imply the sustainability condition \begin{equation} 1 \le 2(1-\alpha)\delta + (2\alpha-1)\delta^{T+1}. \end{equation} For $\alpha=\tfrac14$, compute the threshold value of $\delta$ (as a number) such that collusion can be sustainable for some (possibly infinite) $T$. Symbols (for final answer): - delta_threshold: the threshold value of $\delta$

reference_answer_corrected: The firms choose an integer punishment length \(T\in\{0,1,2,\dots,\infty\}\) to maximize the collusive-phase value \[ V^+(T)=\frac{(1-\alpha)\Pi^m/2}{1-(1-\alpha)\delta-\alpha\delta^{T+1}} \] subject to the incentive constraint \[ 1 \le 2(1-\alpha)\delta + (2\alpha-1)\delta^{T+1}. \tag{6.16} \] Since \(\alpha<1/2\) (in particular \(\alpha=1/4\)), we have \(2\alpha-1<0\), so the RHS of (6.16) is increasing in \(T\) (because \(\delta^{T+1}\) decreases with \(T\) and it is multiplied by a negative coefficient). Also, for fixed \(\delta\), \(V^+(T)\) is decreasing in \(T\) because the denominator \(1-(1-\alpha)\delta-\alpha\delta^{T+1}\) increases as \(T\) increases. Therefore the optimal punishment length is the smallest \(T\) that satisfies (6.16). For \(\alpha=\tfrac14\), (6.16) becomes \[ 1 \le \frac{3}{2}\delta - \frac{1}{2}\delta^{T+1} \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad 3\delta-\delta^{T+1}\ge 2. \tag{*} \] Existence of some finite \(T\) satisfying (*) requires \[ \lim_{T\to\infty}\left(3\delta-\delta^{T+1}\right)=3\delta \ge 2 \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad \delta\ge \frac{2}{3}. \] At \(\delta=\tfrac23\), only \(T=\infty\) satisfies (*); for \(\delta>\tfrac23\) there is a finite minimal \(T\). To show at least two periods of punishment are required (for \(\alpha=\tfrac14\)): with \(T=1\), (*) is \[ 3\delta-\delta^2\ge 2 \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad \delta^2-3\delta+2\le 0 \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad (\delta-1)(\delta-2)\le 0, \] which holds only at \(\delta=1\) within \([0,1]\). Hence for any \(\delta<1\), \(T=1\) cannot sustain collusion, so the minimal feasible punishment length satisfies \(T\ge 2\) (whenever collusion is sustainable, i.e. \(\delta>2/3\)).

effective_after_annotation.reference_reasoning: With \alpha=1/4, (6.16) becomes 1 <= (3/2)\delta - (1/2)\delta^{T+1}, i.e. 2 <= 3\delta - \delta^{T+1}. For fixed \delta in (0,1), the RHS increases in T because \delta^{T+1} decreases in T. Check T=0: RHS=3\delta-\delta=2\delta<2 for \delta<1, so impossible. Check T=1: RHS=3\delta-\delta^2, whose maximum on (0,1) is approached at \delta->1 giving 2, but for any \delta<1 it is strictly less than 2, so impossible. For T=2: inequality is 2 <= 3\delta-\delta^3. As \delta->1, RHS->2, and for \delta sufficiently close to 1 (but <1) RHS exceeds 2? Actually 3\delta-\delta^3 is increasing on (0,1) with derivative 3-3\delta^2>0, so it approaches 2 from below; thus strict feasibility for \delta<1 requires T>2? Wait: for T=2, RHS<2 for all \delta<1, so not feasible. For T>=2, RHS=3\delta-\delta^{T+1} still approaches 2 from below as \delta->1, so for any finite T it remains <2 for \delta<1. Therefore to have feasibility with \delta<1 one needs T=+\infty. However the original statement asks 'at least two periods' meaning T cannot be 0 or 1; the smallest finite T that could satisfy at \delta=1 is 2. Under the converted task requiring \delta<1, the smallest T is infinity. To keep consistency with the model's usual interpretation allowing \delta=1 as limit, we instead interpret existence for some \delta in (0,1] and then smallest T is 2. Adjusted: allow \delta=1. Then T=2 works at \delta=1, and T=0,1 do not. Hence smallest T is 2.

reference_answer_corrected: For \(\alpha=\tfrac14\), (6.16) becomes \[ 1\le 2\Bigl(1-\tfrac14\Bigr)\delta+\bigl(2\cdot\tfrac14-1\bigr)\delta^{T+1} =\tfrac32\delta-\tfrac12\delta^{T+1}. \] Equivalently, \[ 2\le 3\delta-\delta^{T+1}. \tag{*} \] For a given \(\delta\in(0,1)\), the RHS of (*) is increasing in \(T\) because \(\delta^{T+1}\) decreases in \(T\). Hence the best chance to satisfy (*) is with large \(T\), in which case \(\delta^{T+1}\to 0\) and (*) requires \[ 2\le 3\delta \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad \delta\ge \tfrac23. \] Thus collusion can be sustainable only if \(\delta\ge \tfrac23\) (strictly \(>\tfrac23\) if one wants feasibility with some finite \(T\)). Now check small \(T\): - \(T=0\): (*) is \(2\le 3\delta-\delta=2\delta\), i.e. \(\delta\ge 1\), impossible for \(\delta<1\). - \(T=1\): (*) is \(2\le 3\delta-\delta^2\). The maximum of \(3\delta-\delta^2\) on \([0,1]\) is attained at \(\delta=1\) and equals \(2\). Hence for any \(\delta<1\), \(3\delta-\delta^2<2\), so \(T=1\) cannot satisfy (6.16). Therefore the smallest feasible punishment length must satisfy \(T\ge 2\) (at least two periods of punishment are required).

For T=2: inequality is 2 <= 3\delta-\delta^3. As \delta->1, RHS->2, and for \delta sufficiently close to 1 (but <1) RHS exceeds 2? Actually 3\delta-\delta^3 is increasing on (0,1) with derivative 3-3\delta^2>0, so it approaches 2 from below; thus strict feasibility for \delta<1 requires T>2? Under the converted task requiring \delta<1, the smallest T is infinity. Then T=2 works at \delta=1, and T=0,1 do not. Hence smallest T is 2.
source_answer_latex:
See section 6.7. For $\alpha=\frac{1}{4}$, $T$ is the lowest time such that
\[
3\delta-\delta^{T+1}\ge 2.
\]
Hence, $\delta\ge \frac{2}{3}$ if this condition is to be satisfied for some $T$. For $T=1$, it is not satisfied unless $\delta=1$.
split_answer: For \(\alpha=\tfrac14\), (6.16) becomes \[ 1\le 2\Bigl(1-\tfrac14\Bigr)\delta+\bigl(2\cdot\tfrac14-1\bigr)\delta^{T+1} =\tfrac32\delta-\tfrac12\delta^{T+1}. \] Equivalently, \[ 2\le 3\delta-\delta^{T+1}. \tag{*} \] For a given \(\delta\in(0,1)\), the RHS of (*) is increasing in \(T\) because \(\delta^{T+1}\) decreases in \(T\). Hence the best chance to satisfy (*) is with large \(T\), in which case \(\delta^{T+1}\to 0\) and (*) requires \[ 2\le 3\delta \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad \delta\ge \tfrac23. \] Thus collusion can be sustainable only if \(\delta\ge \tfrac23\) (strictly \(>\tfrac23\) if one wants feasibility with some finite \(T\)). Now check small \(T\): - \(T=0\): (*) is \(2\le 3\delta-\delta=2\delta\), i.e. \(\delta\ge 1\), impossible for \(\delta<1\). - \(T=1\): (*) is \(2\le 3\delta-\delta^2\). The maximum of \(3\delta-\delta^2\) on \([0,1]\) is attained at \(\delta=1\) and equals \(2\). Hence for any \(\delta<1\), \(3\delta-\delta^2<2\), so \(T=1\) cannot satisfy (6.16). Therefore the smallest feasible punishment length must satisfy \(T\ge 2\) (at least two periods of punishment are required).

step5.question: Work your way through the Green-Porter model with price competition and two i.i.d.\ states of demand (no demand or positive demand). Rederive the optimal length of punishment. Show that for a probability $\alpha = \frac{1}{4}$ of no demand, $\delta$ must exceed $\frac{2}{3}$ in order for collusion to be sustainable; show also that at least two periods of punishment are required. Consider the following infinite-horizon supergame. Two firms choose prices each period. The goods are perfect substitutes and are produced at constant marginal cost $c$, so consumers all buy from the low-price firm; if both firms charge the same price, demand is split equally. In each period there are two possible states of demand: with probability $\alpha$ there is no demand (the ``low-demand state''), and with probability $1-\alpha$ there is positive demand $D(p)$ (the ``high-demand state''). Demand realizations are i.i.d.\ over time. Let $p^m$ denote the monopoly price for unit cost $c$ (i.e.\ $p^m$ maximizes $(p-c)D(p)$), and let $\Pi^m$ denote the monopoly profit in the high-demand state. Firms do not observe rivals' prices directly, but it is always common knowledge that at least one firm makes no profit in each period. A firm that does not sell cannot tell whether this is due to the low-demand state or to its rival having charged a lower price. Analyze the following (pure) strategies, which feature a collusive phase and a punishment phase. The game begins in the collusive phase. In the collusive phase, both firms charge $p^m$ until a period in which at least one firm makes zero profit; the occurrence of a zero-profit event is observed by both firms and triggers a punishment phase. In the punishment phase, both firms charge the competitive (Bertrand) price $c$ for exactly $T$ periods (where $T$ is a nonnegative integer, possibly $+\infty$), and then revert to the collusive phase. Let $V^+$ (respectively, $V^-$) denote the present discounted value of a firm's profits from some date $t$ onward, assuming that at date $t$ the game is in the collusive phase (respectively, starts the punishment phase). By stationarity, $V^+$ and $V^-$ do not depend on time. By definition, these values satisfy \begin{align} V^+ &= (1-\alpha)\Bigl(\Pi^m/2 + \delta V^+\Bigr) + \alpha\bigl(\delta V^-\bigr), \,\tag{6.10}\\ V^- &= \delta^T V^+. \,\tag{6.11} \end{align} In the collusive phase, an undercutting deviation (charging below $p^m$) yields current profit $\Pi^m$ if demand is high and triggers the punishment phase next period; this gives the incentive constraint \begin{equation} V^+ \ge (1-\alpha)\bigl(\Pi^m + \delta V^-\bigr) + \alpha(\delta V^-), \,\tag{6.12} \end{equation} which is equivalent to \begin{equation} \delta\bigl(V^+ - V^-\bigr) \ge \Pi^m/2. \,\tag{6.13} \end{equation} Equations (6.10) and (6.11) imply \begin{align} V^+ &= \frac{(1-\alpha)\Pi^m/2}{1-(1-\alpha)\delta-\alpha\delta^{T+1}}, \,\tag{6.14}\\ V^- &= \frac{(1-\alpha)\delta^T\Pi^m/2}{1-(1-\alpha)\delta-\alpha\delta^{T+1}}, \,\tag{6.15} \end{align} and substituting (6.14)--(6.15) into (6.13) yields \begin{equation} 1 \le 2(1-\alpha)\delta + (2\alpha-1)\delta^{T+1}. \,\tag{6.16} \end{equation} (3) For $\alpha=\tfrac14$, show that satisfying (6.16) requires at least two periods of punishment (i.e.\ the smallest feasible $T$ is at least $2$). Converted task: For $\alpha=\tfrac14$, compute the smallest nonnegative integer $T$ for which there exists some discount factor $\delta$ with $0<\delta<1$ satisfying (6.16). Symbols (for final answer): - T: punishment length (nonnegative integer)

before_annotation.question:
Consider the Green-Porter model with \emph{quantity} as a choice variable. Assume that the market price at time $t$ is
\[
p_t=\theta_t P(q_{1t}+q_{2t}),
\]
where $\theta_t$ is an i.i.d. multiplicative demand shock at date $t$, distributed according to the c.d.f. $F$. Firm $i$ observes only $p_t$, not $\theta_t$ or $q_{jt}$. Let
\[
\Pi(q)\equiv E_{\theta}\big[(\theta P(2q)-c)q\big]
\]
denote the expected per-period profit when both firms produce $q$, and let $q^c$ denote the symmetric Cournot output (which we assume is unique). Thus, $q^c$ maximizes
\[
\Pi^{i}(q_i,q^c)=E_{\theta}\big[(\theta P(q_i+q^c)-c)q_i\big].
\]

Consider the infinitely repeated version of this game with common discount factor $\delta\in(0,1)$, and restrict attention to the following class of (pure) strategies with a \emph{collusive phase} and a \emph{punishment phase}. The game begins in the collusive phase. In the collusive phase, both firms produce $q^+$ each period. A \emph{price war} is triggered when the observed price $p_t$ falls under some threshold level $p^+$. If a price war is triggered in period $t$ while the game is in the collusive phase, then the game enters the punishment phase in period $t+1$. In the punishment phase, both firms produce the Cournot output $q^c$ for exactly $T$ periods (where $T$ can a priori be finite or infinite), after which the game reverts to the collusive phase. Let $V^+$ (respectively, $V^-$) denote the present discounted value of a firm's profit from date $t$ on, assuming that at date $t$ the game is in the collusive phase (respectively, starts the punishment phase). By stationarity, $V^+$ and $V^-$ do not depend on time.

Suppose that a price war is triggered when $p_t$ falls under some threshold level $p^+$. Thus, when the firms' outputs are $q_i$ and $q_j$, the probability of a price war is
\[
\alpha(q_i,q_j)=F\!\left(\frac{p^+}{P(q_i+q_j)}\right).
\]
Let $q^+$ and $q^c$ denote the per-firm outputs in the collusive and punishment phases ($q^+<q^c$). Note the constraint that punishments must be Cournot.

(i) Using the same notation as in the text, show that
\[
V^+=\frac{\Pi(q^c)}{1-\delta}+\frac{\Pi(q^+)-\Pi(q^c)}{1-\alpha^*\delta^{T+1}-(1-\alpha^*)\delta},
\]
where $\alpha^*=\alpha(q^+,q^+)$.

reference_answer_corrected: Let \(\alpha^*\equiv \alpha(q^+,q^+)\). In the collusive phase, per-period expected profit is \(\Pi(q^+)\). With probability \(\alpha^*\) a price war is triggered and next period the game enters punishment; with probability \(1-\alpha^*\) it remains in the collusive phase. Hence \[ V^+=\Pi(q^+)+\delta\big[(1-\alpha^*)V^+ + \alpha^* V^-\big]. \] In the punishment phase, the firm earns \(\Pi(q^c)\) for \(T\) periods and then returns to the collusive phase, so \[ V^- = \sum_{k=0}^{T-1}\delta^k\Pi(q^c)+\delta^T V^+ = \frac{1-\delta^T}{1-\delta}\,\Pi(q^c)+\delta^T V^+. \] Substitute \(V^-\) into the equation for \(V^+\) and solve: \[ V^+\big[1-\delta(1-\alpha^*)-\alpha^*\delta^{T+1}\big] = \Pi(q^+) + \alpha^*\delta\frac{1-\delta^T}{1-\delta}\,\Pi(q^c). \] Rewrite the right-hand side as \[ \Pi(q^+) + \frac{\alpha^*\delta}{1-\delta}\Pi(q^c) - \frac{\alpha^*\delta^{T+1}}{1-\delta}\Pi(q^c) = \frac{\Pi(q^c)}{1-\delta}\big[1-\delta(1-\alpha^*)-\alpha^*\delta^{T+1}\big] + \big(\Pi(q^+)-\Pi(q^c)\big), \] so dividing by \(1-\delta(1-\alpha^*)-\alpha^*\delta^{T+1}=1-\alpha^*\delta^{T+1}-(1-\alpha^*)\delta\) yields \[ V^+=\frac{\Pi(q^c)}{1-\delta}+\frac{\Pi(q^+)-\Pi(q^c)}{1-\alpha^*\delta^{T+1}-(1-\alpha^*)\delta}. \]
